channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Warhammer 40K 11th Edition Combat Patrol - A Great Playstyle And Introduction For New Gamers
item.content:encoded:
It feels like there has always been war on Armageddon, and it is constantly under threat from Ork hordes. But something has changed, and the scale of the conflict is reaching unprecedented levels. The rest of the universe fares no better, and the Imperium faces threats on all sides from Xenos, mutants, heretics, traitors, and the sheer scale of its own existence. This is Warhammer 40K, and there is only war. 

Warhammer 40K, the sci-fi tabletop game from Games Workshop, launched the 11th edition of its rules recently with the huge Armageddon boxed set. Hot on the heels of this are the individual rules for playing games of 40K, and one of those is Combat Patrol. In this feature, we take a look at what Combat Patrol is, why it's so great for new and veteran players, and what you need to play it.

40,000 Warhammers

Warhammer 40,000 (Warhammer 40K or just 40K) is a tabletop wargame produced by Games Workshop. It first came out in 1987 as Rogue Trader and has grown and expanded, seeing not only many editions of the tabletop game and tie-in tabletop games, but a whole host of fiction about the current era and the history 10 thousand years before, during the events of the Horus Heresy. There are also many video games, those set in the IP, and digital recreations of tabletop games. Merch is also available aplenty, with clothing, action figures, a tie-in release with Magic the Gathering, themed candles, and sticker collections. Games Workshop have also been releasing animated series on their Warhammer+ platform, for Warhammer 40K and their other IPs.

11 Editions Of Gaming Might

Warhammer 40K 11th Edition is the 11th edition of the rules. Starting with Rogue Trader in 1987, it has seen a new edition of the rules every 3 years since 7th Edition in 2014. This edition of the rules has built upon the changes made in 10th Edition, with a focus on changing up how armies are constructed, expanding the number of detachments players use to create their forces, depending on the point size of the game. 11th Edition also sees big changes to terrain and objectives, with terrain footprints now driving objective control, combined with a new missions deck that allows you to assign missions to how your army plays, which means we'll see some big changes in the objective scoring.

40K First Steps

With the last edition of 40K, Combat Patrol, a new way to play was introduced, and it continues with 11th Edition. Combat Patrol is designed to let players get into games as quickly as possible. Players can pick one of the many Combat Patrol boxes available (there are now several for each faction), and play straight away against another Combat Patrol. With single-box purchases, players are able to experience different factions easily, collecting more than one, or testing them out to see which play style they like. 

Games of Combat Patrol use their own datasheets that are free on the Warhammer 40K App, along with the Core Rules, which are available for free on the Warhammer Community downloads page. This means that no purchase is necessary beyond the miniatures and a few tools that we go through below, and you can play Combat Patrol before growing your army and buying the rules for full 40K games.

Combat Patrol First Steps

To play Warhammer 40K Combat Patrol, you will need:

  • Warhammer 40K Rulebook or Free Core Rules download
  • The Combat Patrol datasheets for your Combat Patrol - available in the 40K app (Apple / Android)
  • Your chosen Combat Patrol box, with assembled (and painted if you wish miniatures)
  • Several six-sided dice (D6) and a measuring tool (Either a measuring stick or tape measure)
  • A battlefield, which can be as simple as the floor with some books, or a printed gaming board filled with thematic scenery

The above are the core items you need to be able to play 40K Combat Patrol, but if you want to take your games a little further, 2 other products are releasing alongside the 11th Edition launch waves.

The first is the Warhammer 40,000 Combat Patrol Companion. The Companion doesn't contain any rules required to play Combat Patrol, both the rules and datasheets are available for free as detailed above, but it does contain a huge amount of background for 40K, the different factions and warzones, and lists a lot of the available Combat Patrols and how to expand from them. Veteran players might want the book for the background details, but new players will find useful information on all aspects of the wargaming hobby, and it's a great book to buy alongside your first Combat Patrol.

The second new product is the Warhammer 40,000 Terrain Area Set. This set is a collection of double-sided terrain areas, used to make out the terrain footprints we mentioned above in the 11th Edition changes. This can simply be used to mark terrain areas, with you and your gaming partner agreeing what type of terrain each is when setting up the game, or they can fit under the terrain you have, to clearly show the different terrain footprints on the tabletop. If you're just starting out with 40K, you won't need these straight away, and you might only find they're used in the competitive arena, but they are useful with the 11th Ed changes, and they can be used in place of 3d terrain while you look to build your home gaming tabletop scenery.

Life After Combat Patrol

Once you've played your first few games of Combat Patrol, you might want to expand into full games of 40K, then you will need to pick up the full 40K rulebook and codex for your chosen force, or you might want to try out a few Combat Patrols until you find the faction you like the most. But Combat Patrol will always be there, and I played more games of Combat Patrol than full 40K over the 3 years of the last edition. It's quick to play, so you can get more games in, and you're not punished as hard for mistakes. There's also no meta to chase, and you can just enjoy playing games. Some of the Combat Patrols are stronger and easier to pilot, but overall, they all offer a fun experience. I like being able to jump from faction to faction without having to carry and deploy larger armies all the time, which is still fun, but can be time-consuming when all you want to do is play a few games.

Combat Patrol is a great place to start your 40K journey, but also a great place to continue gaming and exploring factions, and allows you to get several games in over a gaming night, or a quick game in over lunch.

The Warhammer 40K Combat Patrol Companion and Terrain Area Set used to produce this guide were provided by Games Workshop.

Adam is a Tabletop Specialist for TechRaptor. He started writing for TechRaptor in 2017 and took over as Tabletop Editor in 2019 and has since stood down… More about Adam



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/warhammer-40k-11th-edition-combat-patrol-great-playstyle-and-introduction-for-new
